channel.title: NYT > World > Americas
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Runoff in Peru Offers Two Starkly Differing Visions for the Nation
item.description: The presidential election pits Keiko Fujimori, a conservative, against Roberto Sánchez, a leftist, after a crisis marked by delays and protests.
item.dc:creator: Mitra Taj and Genevieve Glatsky
item.media:credit: Sara Wayra for The New York Times
item.media:description: Keiko Fujimori, the leader of the Popular Force Party, at a campaign rally last month in Lima, Peru.

item.title: How Birding Apps Are Boosting Tourism in the World’s Top Bird Destination
item.description: Colombia is home to the most species of birds known to ornithologists. Merlin, an app, is helping to build an “avian tourism” industry there.
item.dc:creator: Max Bearak and Esteban Vanegas
item.media:description: Samantha Giraldo and Bibiana Acevedo looking for birds in Los Guácharos nature preserve in Jardín, Colombia.

item.title: Venezuela Extradites Billionaire Tycoon Alex Saab to U.S.
item.description: The extradition of Alex Saab, who is tied to a huge graft scheme, is part of a purge of powerful figures who helped the deposed president, Nicolás Maduro, stay in power.
item.dc:creator: Simon Romero
item.media:credit: Leonardo Fernandez Viloria/Reuters
item.media:description: Alex Saab, center, in Caracas, Venezuela, in 2023, was once among key allies of the deposed Venezuelan president, Nicolás Maduro.

item.title: How a Times Reporter Found Cartel Gold at the Royal Canadian Mint
item.description: A conversation with Justin Scheck, who found that illegal Colombian gold transformed into legal North American gold through bureaucratic sleights of hand, and ended up in Canada.
item.dc:creator: Shawna Richer
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: This illegal mine near Caucasia, Colombia, sells gold into the Royal Canadian Mint’s supply chain.

item.title: The Push to Indict Raúl Castro, Cuba’s Former President
item.description: With Raúl Castro, Cuba’s former president, possibly facing charges in the 1996 killings, here’s a look back at what happened that day.
item.dc:creator: Frances Robles
item.media:credit: Norlys Perez/Reuters
item.media:description: Former President Raúl Castro of Cuba attending a May Day parade in Havana this month.

item.title: With Possible Raúl Castro Indictment, U.S. Eyes Venezuela Playbook
item.description: Amid stepped-up surveillance flights, a visit of the C.I.A. director and an energy embargo, the White House is trying to increase pressure on Cuba.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes, Tyler Pager, Eric Schmitt, Michael Crowley and Frances Robles
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Stephen Crowley/The New York Times
item.media:description: Raúl Castro is the former president of Cuba and younger brother of Fidel Castro.

item.title: Fugitive Accused of Killing Punk Rock Singer in 1993 Is Arrested in Panama
item.description: Richard Werstine was detained in Panama City last month after more than 30 years on the run, according to the authorities. He is accused of murdering Rodney Barger of the Detroit band Cold as Life.
item.dc:creator: John S.W. MacDonald
item.media:credit: U.S. Marshals Service
item.media:description: Richard Werstine, 56, center, is in Texas on his way to Detroit to face second-degree murder and felony firearms charges, according to prosecutors.

item.title: Photos: Cuba Plunges Into Darkness as U.S. Cuts Off Oil Supply
item.description: The United States has choked off Cuba’s fuel supply, plunging the already impoverished island into an acute energy crisis.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Ramon Espinosa/Associated Press
item.media:description: People playing dominoes by flashlight as a protest fire burns in the background in Havana on Thursday.

item.title: Justice Dept. Aims to Use Terrorism Laws to Target Mexican Officials
item.description: In a new tactic, the Justice Department this week instructed federal prosecutors to build criminal drug cases against Mexican officials using terrorism statutes.
item.dc:creator: Ernesto Londoño, Alan Feuer and Jack Nicas
item.media:credit: Alejandro Cegarra for The New York Times
item.media:description: Flags at the U.S. border with Mexico in Tijuana last year.

item.title: How a Drug Cartel Made a Mexican State Its Tool
item.description: Cartel insiders say that in exchange for bribes and political support they were allowed to operate in Sinaloa state with near total freedom.
item.dc:creator: Paulina Villegas
item.media:credit: Paul Ratje for The New York Times
item.media:description: Cartel gunsmiths in a village on the outskirts of Culiacán, in Mexico’s Sinaloa state, in October.

item.title: Inside the Congolese Hotel Where Trump Deported 15 U.S. Migrants
item.description: They were shackled and sent to Kinshasa by the Trump administration. Now they face a dangerous choice: Go back to Latin America or stay in Africa.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Maclean, Emiliano Rodríguez Mega, Pranav Baskar and Justin Makangara
item.media:credit: Justin Makangara for The New York Times
item.media:description: Hugo Palencia was deported from the U.S. to Kinshasa, the Democratic Republic of Congo, last month.

item.title: Cuba Says It Has Run Out of Oil
item.description: A long-running energy crisis in the island nation reached a breaking point this week, when government officials said they had run out of reserves.
item.dc:creator: Frances Robles
item.media:credit: Lisette Poole for The New York Times
item.media:description: A gas station in Havana this month. In January, the Trump administration cut off oil shipments from Venezuela, which had been supplying a large amount of Cuba’s oil.

item.title: Argentina Races To Find Origin of Hantavirus Outbreak
item.description: The scientific investigation into the origin of cruise infection has become entangled with international finger pointing.
item.dc:creator: Emma Bubola
item.media:credit: Euge Neme/Associated Press
item.media:description: A sign shows information on hantavirus at a hospital in Bariloche, Argentina. A patient with no known connection to the cruise ship is hospitalized there with hantavirus.

item.title: Cecilia Flores Became the Voice of Mexican Mothers
item.description: One of the most prominent activists for Mexico’s disappeared recently found the remains of one missing son. Now she has turned her attention to finding the other.
item.dc:creator: James Wagner and Fred Ramos
item.media:credit: Fred Ramos for The New York Times
item.media:description: Cecilia Flores during a search for her missing son, Alejandro Guadalupe Islas Flores, in Juan José Ríos, Sinaloa, Mexico.

item.title: Judge Orders U.S. to Return Colombian Woman Deported to Congo
item.description: The unusual ruling came after the judge found that the Trump administration had most likely violated the law by deporting the 55-year-old woman to the African country despite its refusal to take her.
item.dc:creator: Pranav Baskar
item.media:credit: Justin Makangara/Reuters
item.media:description: The Colombian woman and other migrants are housed in a hotel outside Kinshasa, the capital of the Democratic Republic of Congo.

item.title: Canada and Alberta Reach Agreement to Raise Carbon Prices as Pipeline Tradeoff
item.description: But the agreement is the latest softening of carbon reduction measures introduced by Prime Minister Mark Carney since he took office last year.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:credit: Pat Kane for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Syncrude facility near Fort McMurray, Alberta, producing synthetic crude oil from oil sands.

item.title: Black Bear Fatally Mauls Uranium Contractor in Northern Canada
item.description: The attack, at a remote uranium mining site in northern Saskatchewan, was only the fourth fatal black bear encounter in the province’s recorded history, officials said.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan
item.media:credit: Victor R. Caivano/Associated Press
item.media:description: The black bear is the most common species in Canada, though bear attacks are rare.

item.title: Alberta Separation Petition Is Dealt a Court Blow
item.description: A judge sided with an Indigenous group that had asked for a petition by separatists to force a vote on the oil-rich province seceding from Canada to be thrown out.
item.dc:creator: Matina Stevis-Gridneff
item.media:credit: Henry Marken/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Separatist leader Mitch Sylvestre is seen during a rally in front of the Elections Alberta headquarters in Edmonton, Canada, this month, as they submit boxes of signatures in the hope of triggering an independence referendum.

item.title: More U.S. Spy Planes are Surveilling Cuba
item.description: As President Trump threatens to act against Cuba, experts are debating whether the flights, which the United States is not taking pains to hide, are just intimidation or foreshadow military force.
item.dc:creator: Frances Robles, Eric Schmitt and Julian E. Barnes
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Ramon Espinosa/Associated Press
item.media:description: A taxi driver in Havana earlier this month.

item.title: U.S. and Mexican Officials Deny C.I.A. Had Lethal Role in Mexico Operation
item.description: The denials came after a CNN report claimed the C.I.A. had been involved in the assassination of a cartel member inside Mexico earlier this year.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes and Maria Abi-Habib
item.media:credit: Carl De Souza/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: “The Government of Mexico categorically rejects any version that seeks to normalize, justify, or suggest the existence of lethal, covert, or unilateral operations by foreign agencies on national territory,” said Omar García Harfuch, Mexico’s security secretary

item.title: Dominican Republic Agrees to Take Third-Country Migrants Deported by U.S.
item.description: The agreement, which reverses President Luis Abinader’s previous stance, is part of President Trump’s efforts to find governments willing to accept deportees.
item.dc:creator: James Wagner and Hogla Enecia Pérez
item.media:credit: Danny Polaco/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: President Luis Abinader of the Dominican Republic previously said, “We do not have, nor will we accept, nor are we obligated to, nor will we accept receiving people from other countries.”

item.title: Quebec Novelist Shines a Harsh Spotlight on Migration, and on Trump’s America
item.description: Thélyson Orélien, a Haitian-born, previously unknown writer, submitted an unsolicited manuscript. Its rights were sold in 23 countries before publication.
item.dc:creator: Norimitsu Onishi and Nasuna Stuart-Ulin
item.category: It Was Either That or Die (Book)
item.media:credit: Nasuna Stuart-Ulin for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Haitian poet and novelist Thélyson Orélien at a café in Montreal’s Côte-des-Neiges neighborhood.

item.title: The Fight to Euthanize Pablo Escobar’s Hippos in Colombia
item.description: Colombia is planning to cull a population of wild hippos, the offspring of the drug lord’s pets, dividing a town where hippos are the main draw.
item.dc:creator: Luis Ferré-Sadurní and Esteban Vanegas
item.media:credit: Esteban Vanegas for The New York Times
item.media:description: A male hippopotamus walking in Doradal, Colombia. Despite their bulk, they can easily outrun humans.
